_NOTE: Reconstruction of Rick v. Brandsema, 2009 SCC 10 facts as a schema instance for fitness-testing the BC FLA Separation Agreement schema. Names and dates are illustrative; the structural assertions are drawn from the SCC reasons (¶¶ 3-25). This instance is intentionally shaped to validate; the substantive Brandsema problem is captured by the values, not by ajv.

solicitor.name: Wife's counsel — found inadequate against vulnerabilities by trial judge

valuation_date.override_reason: Date of the reconciliation agreement under negotiation.

disclosure_complete_attestation.attestation_text: Husband signed a finality / disclosure clause but the trial judge found his disclosure was materially incomplete: the value of corporate and agricultural assets was deliberately understated.

valuation_methodology.basis: Husband's own estimate, no independent appraisal

non_disclosure_acknowledgment_by_other_s.attestation_text: Wife signed an acknowledgment but, as later established, did not in fact know of the undisclosed value at the time of signing.

disclosure_complete_attestation.attestation_text: Wife disclosed her own (more limited) financial information.

non_disclosure_acknowledgment_by_other_s.attestation_text: Husband purported to acknowledge the wife's disclosure was complete.

financial_disclosure_attestations.consequences_of_non_disclosure: FLA s. 93(3)(a): a court may set aside an agreement on grounds including failure to disclose significant property or debts. Brandsema (SCC 2009) treats material non-disclosure as a primary ground for set-aside even where finality clauses are present.

spouse_1.attestation_text: Husband received ILA in advance of execution.

spouse_2.attestation_text: Wife received ILA, but the trial judge found the advice was inadequate against the backdrop of (a) husband's non-disclosure and (b) wife's mental-health vulnerability — the formal box is checked, the substantive adequacy is what later litigation tested.

Wife signed a voluntariness clause, but the SCC effectively held that a clause cannot cure where the other spouse's misrepresentations and the wife's impaired judgment combined to vitiate true free agreement.

equality_of_bargaining_position.assessment: Profound disparity. Husband had operational control of the corporate and agricultural assets; wife had a documented psychiatric condition that the trial judge found impaired her judgment during negotiation.

fla_objectives_referenced: FLA s.84 family property division (default: equal)
miglin_stage_1.departure_from_ssag_explained: Spousal-support quantum based on the unilateral asset valuation; later found unreliable.
miglin_stage_1.departure_from_equal_division_explained: Husband's self-prepared valuation drove the unequal split; the trial judge found the valuation was deliberately understated.

hartshorne_circumstances.why_unequal_division_substantively_fair: The agreement asserted finality and substantive fairness; on review, neither was supported by the record.
hartshorne_circumstances.comparison_to_fla_default_division: Significant departure from the FLA default; the SCC ordered equal division on remand.

substantive_fairness.acknowledgment_of_setaside_grounds: Agreement contained a finality clause; the SCC held a finality clause cannot inoculate a procedural-fairness failure.

entries.decision_label: Reconstructed entry — not a real session
entries.note: This entry exists only to satisfy the schema's instructions_record structure for the fitness test.
